title: Client Emails Never Arrive
hardTitle: EMAILS DISAPPEARING?? several customers said they sent
blurb: Clients say they emailed the account manager, but nothing appeared in her inbox.
hardBlurb: jules says clients keep following up about emails she never got. email seems fine otherwise??

n1.text:
Jules Mercer, an account manager at Elm & Harbor, says three clients mentioned messages she never answered. New internal mail arrives normally, and she can send without error.

"I don't know what I didn't get, which is a very annoying kind of evidence. One client says she wrote Tuesday morning."
n1.hint: Start with one traceable example and determine whether the service accepted, rejected, quarantined, or delivered it.

choices.label: Get one sender address, approximate timestamp, and subject, then run a message trace before changing Jules's mailbox.
choices.feedback: Mail-flow triage starts with a specimen. A trace tells you which layer owns the mystery; without one, every rule deletion and quota change is hopeful vandalism.

choices.label: Check whether Jules's mailbox is full, then increase her quota if it is close.
choices.feedback: Quota is worth checking, but a nearly full mailbox is not the same as a rejected message. Trace first: delivery status keeps plausible causes from becoming assumed causes.

choices.label: Tell Jules to ask all three clients to resend their messages and watch the inbox.
choices.feedback: Resending may reproduce the problem, but it makes clients repeat work before you preserve the original evidence. It also fails to find what else may already be missing.

n2.text:
The trace finds Tuesday's message. Exchange Online accepted it, passed filtering, and delivered it to Jules's Inbox at 09:14. No rejection, no quarantine action. Her mailbox is at 61 percent.

Jules searches the sender and gets nothing.
n2.hint: The service says delivered. Move inward: mailbox rules, folders, archive, and client-side views now outrank transport theories.

choices.label: Review Jules's server-side inbox rules and search all folders, including Archive and Deleted Items, using message details from the trace.
choices.feedback: Accepted and delivered moves the fault boundary into the mailbox. Server-side rules apply even when Outlook is closed, so they come before rebuilding a local profile.

choices.label: Check the tenant quarantine anyway in case the trace status is misleading.
choices.feedback: A quick quarantine check is harmless, but the trace already says the message cleared filtering and reached the Inbox. Follow the strongest evidence rather than restarting the checklist.

choices.label: Recreate Jules's Outlook profile because search must be broken.
choices.feedback: A profile rebuild is expensive and local; it cannot explain a server-side move that also affects Outlook on the web. Check the mailbox from the service side before rebuilding the window onto it.

n3.text: There it is: a rule named "Client filing" moves mail containing "proposal," "invoice," or "revision" into an RSS Feeds subfolder and marks it read. Jules remembers creating something similar during a busy week, but not choosing that folder. Seventeen messages are sitting there.
n3.hint: Recover the mail, preserve enough evidence to explain the impact, and prevent a rushed cleanup from losing messages.

choices.label: Screenshot the rule, disable it, move the 17 messages back in a controlled batch, and have Jules review them before you delete the rule.
choices.feedback: Disable stops new movement; documentation explains scope; controlled recovery avoids turning the Inbox into another mystery. Jules still owns the business decision about which recovered messages need replies.

choices.label: Delete the rule and drag all 17 messages into the Inbox at once.
choices.feedback: It fixes the flow, but erases the configuration before documenting it and creates an undifferentiated pile of read mail. Fast cleanup can make impact review harder.

choices.label: Leave the rule enabled but change its destination to a folder Jules can see.
choices.feedback: A broad keyword rule will keep removing important mail from the Inbox even with a friendlier folder name. When the automation's intent is unclear and impact is proven, disable first and redesign later.

n4.text: Mail flow is normal, and Jules recognizes all 17 recovered messages. Two need same-day replies. She asks, "So next time, what order do you check? Everybody kept saying spam filter."
n4.hint: Give her a plain-language summary and leave the ticket useful for the next technician.

choices.label: Explain: trace one known message first; if rejected, fix transport or quota; if quarantined, inspect filtering; if delivered, check rules and folders. Document the bad rule and recovered-message count.
choices.feedback: That order follows the message inward and prevents random changes. The ticket note turns one solved mystery into a reusable diagnostic path.

choices.label: Tell Jules it was just a bad Outlook rule and close the ticket.
choices.feedback: Accurate, but thin. The client asked for the model, and future support needs the scope and recovery details, not just the final noun.

choices.label: Recommend never using inbox rules again.
choices.feedback: Rules are useful automation; unreviewed broad rules are the problem. Good support teaches safe use instead of banning the feature that happened to be misconfigured.

end_clean.text:
The rule is documented and removed, all 17 messages are recovered, and Jules answers the two time-sensitive clients before lunch. The trace-to-mailbox path is captured in the ticket.

Her closing note: "Mystery solved. Also, RSS Feeds has now received more attention than at any point in its natural life."

end_partial.text:
Some mail is recovered, but the broad automation remains or the investigation stalls before it is safely removed. Jules has a workaround, not confidence, and another client message later takes the scenic route through her mailbox.

The follow-up ticket begins where the first one should have: trace, then filter, then mailbox.
